topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. A formal notification, detailing the publication of my piece, "The Silent Echoes," under the name of a complete stranger. My blood turned to ice, then a slow, burning heat started to spread through my chest. I stared at the screen, the carefully constructed composure of the last few weeks shattering into a million sharp pieces. I didn't cry. Instead, I methodically saved every scrap of evidence—the original submission drafts, the timestamps, the correspondence—into a secure folder. A cold, focused resolve settled over me, hardening my jaw. I started researching the publishing house, tracing every connection, every weak link in the chain. Every late-night search felt like a deliberate strike, each piece of information a calculated step closer to dismantling the deception. I needed to see them sweat.

[story 2]

When I saw the byline, the world tilted. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a calculated theft of my voice. A tight knot formed in my stomach, a pressure building behind my ribs that made breathing shallow. I walked into the editorial office the next morning, my hands surprisingly steady despite the tremor running through my fingers. I didn't speak immediately. I simply placed the printed article on the mahogany desk, the paper feeling heavy with accusation. The editor, a man whose smile never quite reached his eyes, looked up, startled by my intensity. "Is there a problem?" he asked, his tone laced with practiced indifference. I leaned in, my voice low and dangerously level. "There is a problem that requires immediate correction. You need to understand the gravity of what has happened." I watched his facade crack, the flicker of surprise quickly replaced by a defensive hardening.

[story 3]

The realization hit me during my evening coffee ritual. I was scrolling through my professional updates when the headline flashed—my words, perfectly polished, but attributed to someone else. A sudden, violent surge of anger flooded my system, making my vision narrow. I slammed the mug down; the ceramic rattled against the counter. I stood up, pacing the small apartment like a caged animal. Every shadow seemed to hold the shape of the person responsible. I began to move with a coiled energy, not bothering to check the locks or secure anything. I started making calls, my voice clipped and precise, demanding answers. There was no room for pleasantries; only the sharp edge of confrontation. I wasn't looking for an apology; I was looking for accountability, and I intended to extract it piece by painful piece.
